title: Textual #5380 — Mouse input stops after VS Code reload (v2)
subtitle: Recomposed using the 5 OSS-triage convergence types
scope: Textualize/textual issue #5380 and its orbiting archaeology
sourceCoverage: Issue body and comments, inspect_ai workaround commit, closed related issues #5547 / #1171 / #1031 / #5894, linux_driver.py source at revision f8b42bd
objective: Make Textual's mouse input survive a VS Code 'Developer: Reload Window' without requiring the user to restart the app, and decide whether the fix should ship inside Textual or stay as a per-app workaround.

objectiveFacets.name: Root cause
objectiveFacets.description: Synchronization gap between Textual's one-shot mouse-enable ANSI codes and VS Code's xterm.js, which re-initializes without those codes after a reload.

objectiveFacets.name: Proven workaround
objectiveFacets.description: A production-shipped fix in UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai: override _watch_app_focus and re-call _enable_mouse_support() when the app regains focus.

objectiveFacets.name: Shipping path into Textual
objectiveFacets.description: Open PR that ports the inspect_ai pattern into Textual's own App or driver, behind a flag if needed.

objectiveFacets.name: Cross-terminal idempotency
objectiveFacets.description: Claim that re-enabling mouse ANSI codes is safe must hold for tmux, alacritty, ghostty, kitty, Windows Terminal — not only xterm.js.

invariants.name: Mouse-enable ANSI codes are idempotent
invariants.statement: Writing the four mouse-enable ANSI codes again when they are already active is a no-op on xterm.js; assertion has not been independently verified on other terminals.

invariants.name: Mouse is enabled once, never refreshed
invariants.statement: Textual's Linux driver calls _enable_mouse_support() once inside start_application_mode and has no resend path during the app's running lifetime.

sections.title: Symptoms
sections.rationale: Three cards below are reproducible behaviours with attributable witnesses — they need a repro path and an observer name to be trustworthy, which symptom-observation enforces. The generic 'idempotency asserted' claim has no repro path and lives in a separate findings section.

data.name: VS Code reload drops mouse tracking silently

repro_steps.text: 2. Trigger the VS Code command `Developer: Reload Window`
3. Mouse events stop firing in the Textual app; keyboard input continues to work

data.name: tmux session inside VS Code is immune
data.environment: Same VS Code + Textual versions, wrapped in a tmux session

repro_steps.text: 1. Start tmux inside the VS Code integrated terminal
2. Run the Textual stopwatch app inside tmux
3. Trigger `Developer: Reload Window` — mouse continues to work

data.name: App.suspend() followed by resume restores mouse
data.environment: Same repro environment as reload-drops-mouse, after reload

repro_steps.text: 1. Trigger reload so mouse stops working
3. After the block exits, mouse events fire again

sections.title: Findings
sections.rationale: A single card — the idempotency claim — is a synthesized finding (a claim about behaviour, not a specific reproducible observation). It belongs in findings, separated from the repro-backed symptoms.

data.name: ANSI enable codes asserted idempotent (xterm.js only)

notes.text: Inline comment on the inspect_ai workaround: 'ansi codes for enabling mouse support are idempotent so it is fine to do this even in cases where mouse support is already enabled.'
Assertion has only been validated in practice on xterm.js (VS Code). Behaviour on alacritty, kitty, ghostty, Windows Terminal, mintty is untested in this thread.

sections.title: Code anchors — current mouse-enable flow
sections.rationale: Each card pins one exact file and line range in Textual's source at a specific revision. A reader or agent can jump straight to the code without re-grepping, and the pinned revision makes drift visible.

data.name: _enable_mouse_support() — single call site
data.what_it_does: Only call to _enable_mouse_support() inside start_application_mode; never re-runs during app lifetime

why_it_matters.text: This is the synchronization gap — xterm.js reloads have no signal path back here, so Textual never re-sends the enable codes.

data.name: _enable_mouse_support() body — four ANSI codes
data.what_it_does: Writes \x1b[?1000h (VT200), \x1b[?1003h (any-event), \x1b[?1015h (urxvt, deprecated), \x1b[?1006h (SGR extended — what xterm.js uses); then flush()

why_it_matters.text: Any re-enable path added to Textual must write the same codes. Skipping the SGR extended code (?1006h) would leave xterm.js without the coordinate format it reads.

data.name: _disable_mouse_support() — shutdown mirror
data.what_it_does: Writes the `?…l` form of each enable code, called during clean app exit

why_it_matters.text: Force-kill paths skip this; that is the root of the symmetric issue #1171 where coordinates keep leaking to the shell prompt after a forced stop.

data.name: App._watch_app_focus — the insertion point
data.what_it_does: Reactive hook fired when the app gains or loses focus; exists already in Textual, used by inspect_ai's override

why_it_matters.text: The proven fix hooks here. VS Code reload causes the Textual app to regain focus, giving the hook a reliable trigger to re-send mouse-enable codes.

sections.title: Attempts
sections.rationale: Each card names what was tried, what it proved, and — when productionized — where it ships. Probes that only narrowed the cause are marked superseded when a shipped attempt later productionized the same insight.

data.name: Probe: wrap work in App.suspend()
data.cost_to_apply: Disruptive — yanks the whole screen on every restore

what_tried.text: Call `with self.app.suspend(): ...` after the reload to force resume through start_application_mode.

what_proved.text: Confirmed that re-running start_application_mode restores mouse input; this isolated the cause to the mouse-enable path specifically.

data.name: Probe: call driver._enable_mouse_support() directly
data.cost_to_apply: Trivial (one call); but needs a trigger to know when to run

what_tried.text: After a reload, manually invoke driver._enable_mouse_support() on the running app.

what_proved.text: Narrowed the fix to exactly this method. Eliminated the rest of start_application_mode from the picture and established that the ANSI codes alone are what matters.

data.name: Override _watch_app_focus and re-enable on focus gain
data.cost_to_apply: ~20 LOC, App-level override, no invasive refactor; try/except wraps the call

what_tried.text: Override `_watch_app_focus(focus: bool)` on the App subclass. When `focus=True`, call driver._enable_mouse_support() wrapped in try/except.

what_proved.text: The pattern works in production (inspect_ai ships it). xterm.js tolerates repeated enables as documented in the inline comment.
Cross-terminal idempotency remains untested — the patch is safe on xterm.js but has not been exercised on tmux/alacritty/kitty/Windows Terminal.

sections.title: Issue orbit
sections.rationale: Each card is another Textual issue with an explicit relationship to #5380 — same-root-cause, symmetric, adjacent, or prior-art. The shape is a graph of nearby bugs, not a list of findings about the focal issue.

data.name: #5547 — Regression: mouse broken in VS Code (smooth-scroll)
data.relevance: Different root cause — mouse coordinates mis-calculated after commit 9f18dfe (smooth scrolling). Closed by PRs #5549 and #5551. Shares the 'VS Code + Textual mouse' surface but is not the reload-cycle bug.

data.name: #1171 — Coordinates leak to shell prompt after force-stop
data.relevance: The inverse of #5380: when VS Code's debugger force-stops a Textual app, _disable_mouse_support() never runs, so xterm.js keeps emitting mouse coordinates into the next shell prompt. Same state-drift family.

notes.text: Closed without an explicit linked PR

data.name: #1031 — Mouse not working in VS Code Windows terminal (2022)
data.relevance: Older Windows-specific mouse issue in the VS Code terminal. Likely resolved by intervening changes and does not share the reload-cycle root cause.

data.name: #5894 — Mouse capture issues on Windows
data.relevance: Windows mouse capture surface. Worth re-reading only if the cross-terminal idempotency check reveals Windows Terminal specific behaviour.

sections.title: Open debate — shipping path
sections.rationale: The thread contains named positions from the maintainer and the reporter, with a live rebuttal and no resolution. These are stances, not decisions — they may shift, and preserving the exchange stops the next contributor from re-litigating from scratch.

data.name: Will McGugan — 'not ours to fix'
data.position: This is a VS Code bug, not a Textual bug; the reporter should raise it upstream.
data.rationale: xterm.js is the party resetting mouse state on reload; Textual is acting correctly.
data.rebuttal: jjallaire-aisi argued VS Code bugs of this type don't get prioritized; Textual has stronger incentive and a small, idempotent fix.
data.evolution: No follow-up from the maintainer after the rebuttal (as of 2026-04-18).

data.name: jjallaire-aisi — pragmatic ownership
data.stakeholder: jjallaire-aisi (inspect_ai contributor, not a Textual member)
data.position: Even if technically a VS Code bug, Textual should consider landing the fix because VS Code is unlikely to prioritize research into the handshake, and the user failure mode is 'lost work' which is severe.
data.rationale: Fix is small, already validated in inspect_ai, and the cost of the status quo is significant.
data.evolution: User continued to develop workaround in-thread, did not open a Textual PR.

data.name: Status: no PR, no label, no assignee
data.position: The issue is OPEN, has zero linked PRs, no labels, and no assignee.
data.rationale: The technical blocker is not knowledge — the pattern is proven — it is maintainer stance plus an unowned cross-terminal risk check.

data.name: Open question — gate the fix, or apply always?
data.position: If idempotency holds on every Textual-supported terminal, the fix can be unconditional in App base class or driver. If not, it needs to be opt-in (config flag) or gated on detected VS Code (TERM_PROGRAM=vscode).
data.rationale: A PR cannot ship until this question is answered — either empirically (run the check) or by choosing a safe default (opt-in).
data.evolution: No one has run this check yet.
